Yeah, like for us, there's three things that are super important is ability to generate these workflows with coding agents.
So we do the workflow, we do the framework to be self-contained.
So like every workflow is a set of four files at most, and it's structured that way so you can give all the context of that workflow to a coding agent.
So that's the first name.
And like all the other tools, you can spread
And too many files, or they don't have a strong opinion on where the prompts file should go, or where the steps should be.
And another thing that was a challenge for us is that a lot of the frameworks, they are not, they're designed of the code in mind, but not of the execution.
So to us, the hardest part was the runtime.
So we want to run things a thousand times a day for a client, many, many times over.

So, and like, then everybody says, oh, I just deployed this to somewhere else, and, but we know there's a lander, like, super brittle APIs that will fail all the time, they'll back off, they'll break the limit.
So, and that is part of a runtime.
So we do not talk of some existing open source runtime, so the framework wraps around that really well.

So we'll back off automatically, create sub-workflows, so that that execute, that research one, for example, we'll start to create a plan, and that, just that that's a one simple agent that just plans the research, but then we'll run all the executions in parallel, as a single

So workflow on the different agents, and another thing that's super important for us, so first was coding agent optimized, the second one is a runtime super efficient, and the third one is evaluations embedded on it, so we can do evaluation as a CI layer, but also in the execution, LLM as a judge is the core part of the abstraction, and the third one is just everything that runs, we get it as a file system in one place, So instead of having to use like Braintrust, or LaneFuse, or different eval tools, have the data spread everywhere, every execution creates a run file with everything, so we can use that back into the coding agent to optimize the workflow.
So all the things that didn't exist, and it still doesn't, so like every month we do like a research process to like...
we're not trying to build for the sake of building, like, you know, we're very pragmatic, you know, it's just like truly like, we're solving for our own selves, and then it's the problem.
Also doing that, we're just realizing, like, these are primitives that can be used in a lot of different use cases.
And so we are planning to release output.ai, which is our framework, and runtime layer.

So think of it as, like, a version of Divis would be kind of somewhat similar to the studio experience over time, but it's more of, like, you know, lovable-meets-cursor for building workflows and testing and things like that.
It's just not our number one priority this second.
Yeah.
And under the hood, the tech, we might be using open source as well.
Yeah.
Like, the whole fear is that something like Rails came out of Basecamp, for example, because they needed the tech for Basecamp, not to sell Rails.
So same thing with Django and React.
So, and that's the experience that we're having.
Like, we need all these things for ourselves.
There might be a way to do the ecosystem around that, to have a roadmap around that.
But the priority is, like, can we get growthx to do the most efficient agency first?
And I don't know if it's true or not.
I think G said in the early days, you guys were actually building or using Aerofs for a little bit.
And that's where we're, I think.

Currently using HubSpot, I know they're different, but I'm kind of curious, like, what did you see as a jump in terms of, you know, output quality or flexibility going to this own system that you've built compared to something?
Yeah, this is sort of Series A, but I was just going to show you, like, the kind of, like, the issues we kind of ran into, you know, mostly with that.
And you'll probably, like, run into this.
So, for instance, like, Ramp is one of our customers.
They paid for an AirOps tool, Agen, same thing, like, and we still have access to their instance.
They haven't built a single workflow themselves in over a year and don't use it.
I'm not saying there's not a use case, but the difference is I ran a workshop of over 170 people teaching them how to do this stuff.
And out of there's 170 people that pay $500 to come to my workshops, less than 10 are actually building workflows themselves and talking about it publicly, you know.
And for us, like we said before, like, we didn't...
When thinking about fundraising or being a VC-backed company, we were bootstrapped.
when I joined my partners, I'm just going to code all these workflows myself in their ops, and I don't want to write custom code at all, as much as possible.
But we hit the wall in many places, and a big one was maintenance.
And that was funny because that was something that we actually tested at IFTTT during my time there, and we had a pretty large user base, and we did have a drag-and-drop way to create a graph.
IFTTT complex recipes, but a very tiny fraction of people actually stayed active on that.
This is pretty hard to maintain.
So you never cross that gap between a hobbyist or something that's overly simplistic to actually something that's powerful.
And then when I joined Marcel, the complaints were all about quality and the final last mile about things that you need to wrap LLMs around, and how do you build a React agent in their ops.
So you can.
You

And then you can actually do that, but it's so hard to maintain, and then the more of all those you have, like you have to start replicating them, now you have prompts spread everywhere, so like maintenance.

It's really hard, and like we've gone through it, I'll give you a quick example, then I can kind of keep going, but it's like on, this one's a fact checker, like I highly encourage if any of you are in these tools, like to try to rebuild something like this, but this one, what it's doing is it's like splitting the article into chunks, and then extracting passages, and then researching each passage, right?

So it's extracting passages that have claims, and then coming up with both reasoning and questions that it needs to answer in order to verify if it's true or accurate, and then researching each one of these in parallel, and then verifying with evidence if any of these are true or not.
So for instance, like if it's like, I'll try to find one, here we go.
This one, like, did Stanford research analyze nearly 100,000 developers across blah, blah, blah?
And it needs to answer these based on the research and then compile it, and it did all of this in two minutes.
So this would be a process that would probably take, like, two to three hours for one single person to do.
And doing these types of A&T loops, you know, in fact, that would be extremely hard.
That was actually one of the ones that we actually had in our ops back in the day.
And then we could only do three questions, and the graph was just an insane loop of, like, sub stuff.
And now in our code base, it's, like, a folder of four parts.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But hopefully that's helpful as far as kind of, like, and then one quick thing, because I think we get asked this a lot, is just, like, what is our deployment model?
And so since we switched to this deployment model, which is two phases, it's eight-week strategy spread that we call, and then from the pay, and then from there it goes into a 12-month recurring.
And based on the number of work streams they have, so a company like Augment Code has, like, five work streams.
You know, paying us $50,000 a month.
Most, like, earlier stage companies, like Town.com and a few others, are paying us $9,000 to $15,000 for one work stream.
The strategy sprint is $20,000 to $30,000 one time.
Like, Lovable just signed up last week.
They're kicking off this Monday, you know, and it's $25,000 growth execution afterwards for them.

But we just got every single thing down in that eight weeks to really set the right strategies, set up the right workflows, throughout that environment, know, build trust, calibrate, get to publishing and all that, you know.
So we kind of got the process down.
Maybe just on that, not to derail you too much, but, like, what are your sort of initial thoughts on just, like, the repeatability of that deployment motion?

And, you know, I guess I've had sort of a – I've been sitting on a question throughout this, which is sort of, like, the general sort of, you know, how predictable or how sort of uniform the usage is across sort of the product and tools and where you're sort of seeing those areas.
Yeah, so knock on wood, like so far we've had 100% conversion rate on the sprints and honestly like every single cycle is getting easier, better, faster.
And as the platform improves, like that's definitely like improving as well.
We already have a 70% gross margin, but I think one of the big unlocks here is going to be the expert marketplace as well and then continue to invest in all these areas of the platform.
So for instance, like on the platform side, we just started to switch a lot of our workflows to this HM Tech once that we've re-architected, right?
And that's already saving like an insane amount of time, like hours and hours of production and editing, right?

But then also just having the same place where you have the visibility, you're ingesting analytics data, you're ingesting your search console data, you have all the opportunities, all the assignments in one place, all the knowledge base in one place.
Every time we're adding these things because we're solving for ourselves, it's...
It's making the whole delivery process a lot higher leverage.
But then the other piece is we don't think it's going to be a zero forward deployed expert future, but we do think like they're going to continue to have higher and higher leverage, right?

And so the goal is initially we hire them internally because you don't want the person hitting publish for Webflow or SentinelOne, public company, to be a random freelancer with no background check, right?
Like you want a person to be trained and all that as we build trust and get high signal.
But now we're getting to a point where we can start to do an orchestration of that as a marketplace.

So some of the production things and some of the QA and some of the vetting, you know, can happen as like another distributed, you know, the centralized orchestration as opposed to like internal teams only, right?
And that makes sense.
And I think the expert marketplace obviously will itself be a source of information on your customers, right?
Like the sort of the way the activity takes shape.
But you sort of brought up the margin point, like at some
All like things stabilized to like, you know, high 60s, 70s, low 70s with the business.

Like, do you, like you in-house a lot, you have relatively low dependency, right, as a business, but like where, like what are your thoughts on just like the pricing of all of these, like vendors' inputs behind the product?
Like how durable do you think they are?
Yeah, yeah.
It's cost.
So I think one thing that is perhaps a little bit counterintuitive that we're seeing is that our service to investors looks expensive, to customers looks cheap.
Like literally, like when people say, I'll give you an example, like one of our customers, Tavern AI, they got a quote from this agency called Graphite for $500,000 for the year.
And they're paying us, I believe, $9,000 or $12,000 a month, right?
They're like, this is insane.
You guys are going to, they, like essentially told us $500,000 and they were going to do briefs and they were.
We're going to do all the last mile execution.
And there's so much of that happening there in services that we are delivering higher quality, higher speed, and better pricing per output, right?
And so for them, it's kind of a no-brainer, but they also get to work with people that normally agencies wouldn't have access to, like our chief content officer, like our directors.
These are people that, like, you would have to pay $300,000, $400,000 a year to get, you know, for yourself.
And agencies can't afford that either.
The way we're thinking is that, our whole thesis is that LLMs plus APIs enabled services often in the sense of, like, one person can perform as an entire team if that person has the right tools.
So our fact with growthx is essentially, like, this is our pilot to see if we can build that tool set.
And, like, so far we've been able to shrink the amount of time and the amount of people required for every part of the process.
And as as we...
We get better and better at that, then we can start opening up things for the client to do that themselves if they want to.
But so far, what actually the client really want to do is just have more visibility to the analytics, the more visibility to the work that's getting done, but at the final point.

They don't want to do the workflows themselves, they don't want to write the content themselves yet, they just want to get the output out, and that kind of validates the way we're thinking, that outcomes is what really matters, and by us setting up the company this way, we can move a lot faster, because the SLA and SLO is for our own team.
So if we're changing models, for example, I can tell the entire user base, our 50 employees, hey, this morning, we're off.
So I pre-preload all your work before, and we're going to be down for four hours, and we're to be testing everything out.
You could never do that at a company like IFTTT or Basecamp, for example.
So we can move faster.
One quick question on the marketplace model you're envisioning.
Is that going to be still managed by you?
No.
Or is it going to be a true marketplace where people will have their own rates and the customers will choose who they're working with?
We essentially don't know yet.
That's the short version of the answer.
And like today, like the marketplace is an internal need for ourselves.
So we have a high leverage, high bar folks setting up a workspace and a strategy space.
Those folks don't think they're going to go in the marketplace anytime soon.
But then we have 40, 50 people doing the day-to-day operations of the account.
domain experts.
Domain experts.
We had an article this morning for Clerk about SSO.
It's like we can't expect an editor to be an expert in SSO.
Like you guys could just find those experts and shop them out and your client doesn't know.
Exactly.
Or have a marketplace where they're looking through and selecting somebody.
Right.
That's what we're going to do.
It's going to be called expertlayer.com.
And so there is like an opportunity to be an API for experts, you know, that that models can use.
But that's not the V1, I don't think, you know.
The V1 was essentially to be move all over our feet.

50 folks to that environment so they don't have to be attending Slack meetings or like huggles with the client and stuff like that, and they can focus on what they do the best, but after that, then we can start opening up for clients and other folks to do that.

Yeah, like, and keep in mind, like, just for a little context for folks, like, I was at Skill.ai for two years, and then after that at DeepRam, almost two years as well, and we train our own models as well, and, you know, and so, and had our own data labeling operations internally, so, so I, I've seen kind of like the, every flavor of that, and Serge.ai is a strategic partner and our biggest customer, and, and so there's also a lot of things we're exploring there, and so we just learn how to scale the, you know, human intervention expert preference data, you know, the difference is, I think these companies like Merkur and others are getting paid hundreds of millions of dollars to generate fake or synthetic expert data, meaning, like, these aren't real experts, but they're not in a real work environment.
They're.

We're making up prompts and then vetting that what we're doing instead of starting with service and we believe the byproduct of this is going to be highly valuable frontier expert data, you know, except we're getting paid to generate the data, we're getting paid to do the work and this data, you know, we're backing into the data as opposed to turning, starting with a data labeling company, you know, so that's part of the opportunity, you know, that we have in front of us as well.

In addition to building this common core that can then be applied to, you extractive apps, like CheckThat.ai that we're launching very soon, like in about a month, you know, which we can go into it later, but then also adjacent services that we're constantly validating, for instance, for Okta and OnZero, we're doing sales, sales enablement and prospecting, you know, so we're very thoughtful to have like more of an 80-20, 80-20 focus on like the core of what we're doing, 20% to continue to kind of expand the frontier of what we're doing.
Even internally, we're using our workflows for hiring, recruiting, and a bunch of other things already, you know, and so we see a lot of, like, potential there.
I know we're out of time, but I think just a quick, like, vision question here.
We talk a lot about this internally.
You mentioned it, right?
Like, nobody loves working with their agencies.

Like, I know you're kind of breaking this model a bit, but, like, I guess long-term, like, do you see this AEO space playing out similarly to how, like, SEO played out with this long tail of agencies?
Right?
That, like, everybody hates and, like, you guys are the breakout?
I guess, yeah, how do you see this individual kind of playing out?
Either similar?
There's no, like, if you look at the 32,000 agencies in the U.S.
alone that do content in SEO, right?
And you look at tools like Samrush and others, like, the reason for that is because when tools fall short of execution and actually driving results, services have to make up the rest.
Right?
And that's why you have that.
And so I think the same thing is going to happen with AEO with the current solutions is like visibility is not like a one click.
You have like Jasper and others that try to do like the full, put the whole burden on the company to try to figure out A to Z.
And so I think AEO, what's happening is like there is a lot of overlap with SEO and high quality content, but there's things that kind of play to our advantage because the content needs to be fresh.
They're way more biased towards that, fact-checked, you know, and the volume needs to be higher because you just have a longer tail, right, of topics that you need to be able to cover.

And so it's becoming an accelerant to our business, but even if that wasn't a trend, our business would still be relevant because content is still the atomic unit, you know, forever for marketing, you know.
But services, like one way to think about it is like we're building a common core, GrowthX and the iteration you see on our website is one service around marketing and content.
And you have adjacent services here, you have self-serve.

modules, if you will, like check that.ai, there's a marketplace play here as well, but as well as like additional services, like, so think of like Tiny, the public company, right, that has like a bunch of, it's almost like a PE play in some way, or accelerator play, it's just like, we don't emphasize that at this stage, because like, we're, we're not demand constraints, you know, and so like, we're really trying to execute methodically, while building the right foundation.
Got it.
Yeah.

I know we've, yeah, we've done a lot here, and kind of already at the time, maybe if could just briefly share a little bit about the goals for the rest of the year, and next year, kind of like how you're looking at forecast, and I know you're kind of on the side here, which is perfect, and then.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.

Yeah, so, we're raising 40 to 60 million, we've, just on timeline really quickly, like we have a second partnership meeting today, and then another one on Friday, and a couple more being scheduled, so we're kind of like, already have a few folks.
Thank
Leaning in, but we're really excited about potentially bringing in, like, you know, you all and a few others that we're talking to.
Our goal for this year is to hit about $16 million.
And right now we're artificially, if you will, bottomacting ourselves by only doing two kickoffs a week.
We're booked through the end of September.
We've done two kickoffs a week the whole year.
We've just started hiring sales, right, haven't invested in go-to-market.
And so our goal is to really double down and go-to-market, to not only rely on word-of-mouth and, you know, and kind of organic growth alone.
And then we're launching CheckThat.ai as a way to completely commoditize the visibility layer by, think of it as, like, crunch-based meets g2.com for visibility.
And so we'll share more soon.
And so for our goal of next year, hitting 47, just to give you an idea, we have to go from two kickoffs a week to about...
For starting in Q2 next year to hit that number.
And so we already have more than two a week demand.
And so we feel really, really good.
And so these projections are pretty like, if you dig into the numbers, we have pretty good confidence here.
And this is without us investing in sales or outbound or anything like that.
And so if you look at some of these other players, they've invested very heavily in sales and outbound and marketing and doing ads and things like that in order to grow revenue.
And so for us, like we did the opposite.
We figure out how to deliver first.
And now like we can pour gasoline on the go-to-market engine.
Love it.
And yeah, any updates on the round?
still meeting with potential leads right now?
Yeah, yeah.
Like we, honestly, like for us, like I don't think we are the investment for everyone, to be honest, because people try to pattern match us to too many things.
Like, are you SaaS?
Are you this?
Are you that?
You know?
And so for us, it's more about like...
that's...
And
Finding the right strategic partner that gets it and doesn't try to, like, change us into BSS, right?

Like, we're building a very different type of AI native, like, company that I think more companies are going to end up doing this, where you start with revenue, you work way backwards to delivering the right outcomes that customers actually give a  about, and then you build a strong common core, and you figure out what are the other ways to deliver, you know, value to customers in different markets in these cases, you know?
Yeah, agreed.
Yeah, I mean, I was just getting more on just on timeline, and was there anything we needed to know in terms of, yeah, allocation?

No, I think, like, we've already told a lot of our investors we're talking to that we are talking to you all and a few others, you know, that are just, like, more strategic in as far as, like, and a lot of overlap with the type of customers we have and want to continue to get, you know?
And so I think they're all, like, super, and our board as well, super supportive of considering that, you know?
I think last round we did it.
And add almost any angels or any additional investors, just because we want to get back to work super fast.
But this round, we're a lot more open to making sure we have enough allocation to bring in strategics, you know.
Well, thank you.
We can work quickly, as I mentioned.
I think from here, you know, maybe getting a couple of customer perspectives, either like indirectly or directly would be really, really helpful.
You know, often we do share that activity with the lead investors.
We're not like double-hitting folks.
And you have so many good customers on that list that we do know as well, so we can probably get some perspectives.
That would be helpful.
And then we, if helpful, we can also share our template side letters so you can get a sense of like, just like the things on our side.
It's pretty straightforward, but wouldn't want any, you know, surprises on that end.
So those would be like the next steps I think I would suggest.
And I apologize if I should know this, but we shared the data room with you all, right?
Yep, we have it.
Okay, cool.
There's a page there with lists of customers that, so anyone on that list you want to know.
Intro, a warm intro, let us know.
Just because these GSD, whatever services, like, they're kind of garbage and they'll hit up the wrong people and spam.
A CMO's not going to take a call from them, you know, so if you want an intro, we're more than happy to make those intros.
So there's a list there just to make your lives easier, you know.
And we've already prepped our customers saying, hey, we're really sorry.
I know you're going to get a lot of inbound from investors right now.
they're like, it's totally okay with it, you know.
I appreciate it.
And as I said, like, I think at right time, I'd love to get you in front of the web lead at HubSpot as well.
You know, I think it's a little different perspective because they are sort of more hands-on and building things.
But I think, you know, I think it would be great to get their eyes on this.
And obviously, you're serving other public companies.
So I think it would be a great next step as well just to get.
Perfect.
Yeah, that sounds great.
And if anything else comes up, like, let us know.
We'll keep you all posted on the timeline as well.
And then as we kind of get closer to a lead, then, like, we'll share that.
right right back.
Make intros, but I'm pretty sure everyone that we're talking to are folks that you either co-invested or know very well.
So no surprises there, I don't think.
Well, thank you so much for the time.
Great to meet you, Daniel.
Yeah, likewise.
Thanks, team.
Talk soon.
Thank you.
Appreciate the time.
you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[HubSpot Ventures team meets with GrowthX founders to discuss their AI-powered content marketing platform and potential investment.](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=73.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX has built an end-to-end AI-powered content marketing platform with $11M ARR, targeting $16M this year and $47M next year](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=643.0)
  - [Their model combines AI workflows, expert marketplace, and managed services to deliver high-quality content at scale](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=665.0)
  - [Raising $40-60M Series A; in discussions with potential lead investors and strategic partners like HubSpot](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2864.0)
  - [Strong customer traction and unit economics; 70% gross margins with plans to improve further](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2360.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Platform Overview

  - [Built custom AI framework and runtime for content workflows](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1715.0)
  - [Combines strategy, AI workflows, expert oversight, and last-mile execution](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1319.0)
  - [Platform includes knowledge base, artifact management, and workflow engine](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1461.0)
  - [Fact-checking and research capabilities powered by parallelized AI agents](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1715.0)

### Business Model and Traction

  - [$50k/month for larger customers like Augment Code (5 workstreams)](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1945.0)
  - [$9-15k/month for earlier stage startups (1 workstream)](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1945.0)
  - [$20-30k upfront strategy sprint, then 12-month recurring contract](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1715.0)
  - [100% conversion from strategy sprints to recurring contracts so far](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2222.0)
  - [Artificially constraining growth to 2 new customer kickoffs per week](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2894.0)
  - [No outbound sales or marketing spend to date; mostly word-of-mouth growth](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2974.0)

### Team and Expertise

  - [Founders have deep experience in content, growth, and AI from companies like IBM, HP, HashiCorp](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=287.0)
  - [Engineering team focused on AI-native, experienced developers](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1715.0)
  - [Building out expert marketplace to scale domain expertise](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2537.0)

### Competitive Positioning

  - [Positioned as disrupting marketing services, not building a SaaS tool](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1284.0)
  - [Significantly cheaper than traditional agencies while delivering higher quality](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2360.0)
  - [AI enables small team to perform work of much larger agency](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=1715.0)

### Future Vision

  - [Launch CheckThat.ai as commoditized visibility layer](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2866.0)
  - [Expand into adjacent services like sales enablement](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2866.0)
  - [Build "common core" technology applicable to multiple use cases](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2866.0)
  - [Potential to generate valuable frontier expert data as byproduct](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=2641.0)

## Next Steps

  - [HubSpot Ventures to review customer list and request warm intros for references](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=3121.0)
  - [GrowthX to keep HubSpot updated on fundraising timeline and lead investor discussions](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=3175.0)
  - [Potential intro to HubSpot web team for product feedback](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=3149.0)
  - [HubSpot to share template side letter for review](https://fathom.video/share/YVrFGpFJ_6mMz1JfbavysVcrrSdL1pyQ?tab=summary&timestamp=3101.0)

action_items.description: Identify & reach out to select GrowthX customers from data room list for due diligence perspectives
Share HubSpot Ventures template side letters w/ GrowthX team
